brief.buyerJob: Reconcile instant-payment authentication, exception handling, and audit evidence across product, risk, and compliance before the Q3 roadmap freeze.
brief.currentWorkaround: Separate spreadsheets, rail-specific dashboards, and manual Slack escalations.
brief.hiddenRisk: Unclear ownership when exceptions touch both SCA / step-up authentication and funds-flow operations.
brief.trigger: Situation trigger: Q3 roadmap freeze. Money20/20 Europe is the occasion, not the buyer's reason to care.
notes: No approved proof; strict no-invention mode in effect.
No assets; no attachments, links, one-pagers, or worksheets referenced.
Sender attendance, booth, agenda track, and availability unknown; no meetup CTAs, no track names, buyer-timing CTAs only.

vp_director_head_of_payments.personaLabel: VP / Director / Head of Payments at fintechs and digital banks

touches.subject: step-up owner
touches.body: {{first_name}}, when a step-up decision at {{company}} changes the payment state, the owner can get blurry: product sees authentication, risk sees funds movement, and payments ops gets the exception. How are you deciding who owns that handoff when SCA, FedNow, RTP, and SEPA Instant all have different evidence trails? The expensive part is not the exception itself; it is the unresolved owner when Q3 roadmap tradeoffs start. Is this something you are trying to settle before the freeze, or parked for later?

pain_angle.label: step-up owner
pain_angle.sourcePain: SCA step-up owner unclear when auth changes payment state
pain_angle.mechanism: authentication changes the payment state and ownership blurs
pain_angle.costOfInaction: Q3 roadmap tradeoffs start without a named owner

touches.subject: evidence pack
touches.body: {{first_name}}, audit evidence at {{company}} can become a scavenger hunt when the auth log, rail trace, and case notes live in different systems. How quickly can your team reconstruct why a payment moved after authentication changed? If that evidence pack still takes manual stitching across FedNow, RTP, SEPA Instant, and cards, worth a closer look?

pain_angle.label: evidence pack latency
pain_angle.sourcePain: audit evidence lives in rail dashboards and auth logs that do not line up
pain_angle.mechanism: case notes, auth logs, and rail traces require manual stitching
pain_angle.costOfInaction: the review takes longer than the exception itself

touches.subject: roadmap queue
touches.body: {{first_name}}, this is usually the week new rails get attention, but the roadmap queue at {{company}} may still treat auth-and-payments cleanup as ops work. Where is that sitting on your side now: a Q3 product item, a risk cleanup, or something nobody owns yet?

pain_angle.label: roadmap queue
pain_angle.sourcePain: payments-auth work can miss Q3 roadmap when it is treated as ops cleanup
pain_angle.mechanism: rail work gets classified as cleanup instead of product scope
pain_angle.costOfInaction: another quarter passes before a named product owner exists

touches.subject: after the notes
touches.body: {{first_name}}, after the event, the hard part at {{company}} is usually turning vendor notes into one ownership decision. If the same auth-change work is still split between payments ops, product, and risk, the cost is another quarter of reviews that nobody can confidently close. What is your working answer now: product-owned, risk-owned, or parked until the next rail change?

pain_angle.label: vendor-note translation
pain_angle.sourcePain: vendor notes do not translate into ownership decisions
pain_angle.mechanism: event takeaways need to become one owner and one roadmap decision
pain_angle.costOfInaction: another quarter of reviews that nobody can confidently close
